coredata.dc:title: Citrus hystrix peels derived carbon dots based colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent sensors for highly selective detection of Al3+ in the presence of Zn2+ in water and processed food
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pandi, Archana
Chinnathambi, Sekar
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), synthesized from Citrus hystrix peels, exhibited maximum fluorescence emission at 420 nm when excited at 330 nm. Along with Quercetin (QT), the CDs quenched the emission at 420 nm upon the addition of aluminium (Al3+) ions and the combination also yielded a new strong emission peak at 480 nm. The intensity of the emission peak at 480 nm increased with the increase in Al3+ concentration which is accompanied by a visible colour change of the solution from colourless to yellow. The formation of QT-Al3+ complex in the system results in the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) causing the change of blue fluorescence emission to green. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor has been constructed using CD-QT system which exhibits a great potential towards selective determination of Al3+ by overcoming the limitations possessed by single wavelength detection probes. The proposed sensor detects Al3+ over a wide range of 5–550 µM with the lowest detection limit of 0.695 µM. Also, this sensor hardly shows any vulnerability towards the most possible interfering Zinc ions and other potential metal ions. The proposed method has been successfully applied for the determination of Al3+ leached from aluminium food wrapper and cooking utensil with high selectivity. Excessive leaching of Al3+ into food has been evidenced while cooking in acidic conditions using this sensor. The fabricated fluorescent sensor has been used to detect Al3+ in sediments collected from large scale wheat storage warehouses, where aluminium phosphide is used as fumigant for pest control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Quercetin
Aluminium leaching
Colorimetric detection
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from Citrus hystrix peels
Ratiometric and colorimetric detection of Al3+ using CD-QT system
Application of sensor in real sample analysis
Tap water analysis by standard addition method
Al3+ residuals in wheat grains from aluminium phosphide (fumigant)
Al3+ leached into water during cooking
Al3+ leached into water during cooking in acidic condition

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)&(b) TEM images of prepared CD; (c) FTIR spectrum of aqueous CD solution; (d) XPS survey spectrum of CD; Deconvoluted (e) C1s spectrum; (f) O1s spectrum.

(a) UV absorption spectrum of CD at 280 nm and Emission spectrum of CD excited at 420 nm; (b) Emission spectra of CD excited at different excitation wavelengths; (c) Emission spectra of CD in PBS at different pH ranging from 4 to 9; (d) Overlapping of UV absorption spectra of QT and QT-Al3+ with emission spectra of CD.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of QT with different concentrations of Al3+; (b) Linear calibration of F485 versus concentration of aluminium; (c) Emission spectra of CD-QT system upon addition of various concentrations of Al3+ (0–550 µM); Insert shows the images of CD-QT system in the presence and absence of Al3+ under UV light; (d) Linear calibration of F480/F420 versus concentration of aluminium; (e) Colour change of the CD-QT system with the addition of various concentrations of Al3+(0–550 µM).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of QT- 50 µM Al3+ with and without the presence of CD; (b) Emission spectra of QT-250 µM −Zn2+ and QT-50 µM- Al3+ (without CD − dotted lines) and (with CD − solid lines); (c)&(d) Fluorescence spectra of CD-QT system with different metal ions.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations of Al3+ in tap water in the CD-QT system; (b) Emission spectra of different concentrations of aluminium phosphide treated wheat grains residual in CD-QT system; Emission spectra of different volume of Al foil in deionized water cooked for (c) 15 min and (d) 20 min; In the presence of lime juice (e) 15 min and (f) 20 min.
Comparison of the credits of sensors fabricated for Al3+ detection by employing various fluorescent probes.

row.entry: Sulfonated derivatives of salicylaldehyde S1 and SA1
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots catalyzed by Fe3+
Citrus hystrix peels derived) Carbon dot – Quercetin system
Aluminium Foil used in food packaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Al3+ in tap water using the proposed CD-QT system.
Quantification of Al3+ using the CD-QT system in deionized water collected after heating in a pressure cooker containing Al foil.

head.ce:title: Citrus hystrix peels derived carbon dots based colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent sensors for highly selective detection of Al3+ in the presence of Zn2+ in water and processed food

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioelectronics and Biosensors, Alagappa University, Karaikudi 630001, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bioelectronics and Biosensors, Alagappa University, Karaikudi 630001, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CD – Carbon dot; QT – Quercetin; IFE – Inner Filter Effect.

Carbon dots (CDs), synthesized from Citrus hystrix peels, exhibited maximum fluorescence emission at 420 nm when excited at 330 nm. Along with Quercetin (QT), the CDs quenched the emission at 420 nm upon the addition of aluminium (Al3+) ions and the combination also yielded a new strong emission peak at 480 nm. The intensity of the emission peak at 480 nm increased with the increase in Al3+ concentration which is accompanied by a visible colour change of the solution from colourless to yellow. The formation of QT-Al3+ complex in the system results in the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) causing the change of blue fluorescence emission to green. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor has been constructed using CD-QT system which exhibits a great potential towards selective determination of Al3+ by overcoming the limitations possessed by single wavelength detection probes. The proposed sensor detects Al3+ over a wide range of 5–550 µM with the lowest detection limit of 0.695 µM. Also, this sensor hardly shows any vulnerability towards the most possible interfering Zinc ions and other potential metal ions. The proposed method has been successfully applied for the determination of Al3+ leached from aluminium food wrapper and cooking utensil with high selectivity. Excessive leaching of Al3+ into food has been evidenced while cooking in acidic conditions using this sensor. The fabricated fluorescent sensor has been used to detect Al3+ in sediments collected from large scale wheat storage warehouses, where aluminium phosphide is used as fumigant for pest control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aluminium garners a pivotal role in countless applications and thus its adverse environmental effects are the burgeoning issue to be taken into consideration. Excessive levels of aluminium ions (Al3+) in the human body resulting from dialysis fluids, medications, flocculating agent in water treatment, are responsible for various neurological disorders. Not only does Al3+ from drinking water and medications accumulate in body, but also those leached into food while cooking from the aluminium utensils or aluminum foil wrappers can be easily absorbed by the human body to a good extent [1]. Aluminium pressure cookers are one of the most commonly used cook wares in recent days. Moreover, aluminium foil is being used for food packing due to the advantages like grease proof, flexible sizing, cost effectiveness and excellent malleability for construction of any shape. Aluminium foil has been extensively used not only in food packaging, but also in various cooking procedures, like baking, grilling etc. Especially during grilling of few foods, in addition to many spices, acidic ingredients like vinegar, lemon, citric acid, and tamarind are added and wrapped using aluminium foil and subjected to direct heating. Such cooking procedures using aluminium foil in acidic conditions, especially in the presence of highly Al complexing ligand groups like citric acid enormously contributes to the leaching of Al3+ ions into the food to a greater extent.

Metal phosphides like aluminium phosphide and zinc phosphide are generally used as fumigants in large scale grain storage warehouses. Post harvest treatment of stored grains involves the distribution of these tablets or powders directly to the grain mass, which renders the purging of the resulting aluminium hydroxide sediments after fumigation arduous. Although many studies have been reported about the toxicity of the phosphine gas that evolved from the fumigant and the phosphine residual on the grain surface, there are no reports concerning the toxicity of the aluminium residual powders on the grain surface resulting from fumigation reaction. However, washing the grains before utilizing them could possibly remove the Al3+ present, yet during large scale fluor production using the stored grains can still carry the Al3+ in it [2].

It is to be referred that World Health Organization (WHO) prescribed the weekly dietary intake of more than 7 mg of Al3+ per kilogram of body weight by an adult result in consequences like softening of bones by interfering with calcium absorption, anemia by affecting iron absorption, or neurodegenerative disorders [3,4]. Thus, determination of Al3+ traces in food and environment remains as the foremost task because it stands a root cause for severity of dementia like Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s diseases, renal osteodystrophy, etc.[5]. Numerous conventional methods like atomic absorption spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry have been used to quantify Al3+ concentration in aqueous samples. However, these standard methods have disadvantages such as high instrumentation cost, prolonged time for detection, requirement of trained manpower for operation and incompatible for on-site detection. On the other hand, fluorescent sensor holds a few unique advantages like high sensitivity, selectivity, real-time monitoring, low cost and quick measurement and suitability for on-site investigation [6].

Zou et al., 2014 employed a blue emission CDs derived from chemical precursor such as citric acid and diethylenetriamine to develop a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Al3+ detection with a fair detection range of 1–60 µM, yet it suffers interfering effect of Zn2+ ions [7]. Wang et al., 2020 developed a red emission CDs by solvo-thermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine in methylbenzene for ratiometric detection of Al3+ with narrow detection range of 0–35 µM [8]. The majority of the reported sensing platforms for detection of Al3+are based on either synthetically derived fluorogenic probes or the detection system is governed via a single wavelength-based response. Such single wavelength based sensor suffers from high interference effects by the competing species and makes them vulnerable to selectivity. Especially, interference of certain metal ions like Zn2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ during the detection of Al3+ continues to be a serious problem because of strong chelation of these ions with majority of the fluorescent probes which causes major difficulty in accurate detection. Moreover, Single wavelength based sensors are vulnerable to excitation intensity, experimental conditions and high background noise which results in low reproducibility and accuracy. By contrast, the ratiometric fluorescence sensors possess a high signal to noise ratio and eliminate the above mentioned limitations [9]. Thus, there is an urgent need to design and develop a ratiometric fluorescent sensor, preferably using carbon dots synthesized by a simple green approach without subsequent surface modifications by heteroatom doping for precise detection of Al3+ over a wider range.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of spherical or quasi spherical carbon material constituting polyaromatic carbon core shell enveloped with amorphous carbon domains, has intrigued the researchers towards its potential competence to conventional semiconductor quantum dots due to their equivalent optical properties[10,11]. The strong photoluminescence of CDs is due to the synergistic effect of the conjugated graphitic core and the surface rich in functional groups. CDs replace traditional quantum dots because of their unique luminescence performance, smaller size and high photostability against photo-bleaching. Further, the excitation dependent emission behavior of CDs endows multicolor tunable emission property throughout visible and NIR region which makes it competent like the mechanochromic fluorescent dyes [12]. Thus, CDs bestow various applications in the fields of bioimaging, biosensing, optoelectronics, and fluorescent sensors. Generally, CDs have been obtained by standard methods like etching of larger carbon materials, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, thermal oxidation, microwave irradiation, hot injection, and pyrolysis. However, these methods suffer several limitations such as limited spectral efficiency, low product yield, lack of size control, and the use of toxic chemicals for experiments [13,14]. Therefore, an affordable and green approach like hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from biomass wastes has been explored recently [15]. In particular, biomass sources rich in carbon contents like carbohydrates, lipids, minerals and phenolic compounds are reported to be attractive precursors for carbon dots synthesis. Thus, bio wastes like orange peels, mango peels, rice husk, banana peels, lemon peels, lychee seeds, walnut peels, papaya juice etc., have been used in the synthesis of carbon quantum dots [16–20].

In the present work, we have chosen Citrus hystrix peels as a biomass precursor to synthesize CDs, because it contains variety of terpenes such as citronellal, as its major component in addition to ß- pinene, limonene, and sabinene which serves as excellent sources of carbon. CDs derived from Citrus hystrix peels by hydrothermal method have been used to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescent sensor along with QT. The synthesized CDs were found to possess high quantum yield, intense emission without requiring extrinsic heteroatom doping and excitation dependent emission property.

Citrus hystrix peels were collected from a local market. Quinine sulfate, Quercetin dihydrate and absolute ethanol were purchased from Central Drug House (P) Ltd. The solutions of metal ions were prepared from analytical reagent grade chemicals (Pb(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Zn(NO3)2, HgCl2, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, MnCl2, Al(NO3)3·9H2O)and used without any further purification.

The TEM images of CDs were acquired from JEOL JEM 2100 High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM). The FTIR spectrum was recorded in IR-Affinity Shimadzu. Elemental composition and the purity of CDs were ascertained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy using PHI – VERSAPROBE III (Japan). Absorption studies were carried out using UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific) − Evolution 201. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using JASCO FP8300 Spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) which uses the Xe lamp as excitation light source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from Citrus hystrix peels
ce:section.ce:para: Citrus hystrix peels were washed with deionized water several times and sun dried for 24 h. The sun-dried peels were carbonized in hot air oven at 150◦C for 10 h. The carbonized peels were ground into a fine powder using mortar and pestle. 4 g of this pretreated Citrus hystrix peel powder was washed with 150 ml of aqueous 0.1 M concentrated H2SO4, rinsed with water for several times, filtered and finally dried in a hot air oven at 150◦C for 2 h [13]. To the obtained Citrus hystrix peel powder, 150 ml of deionized water was added and placed in a Teflon lined autoclave and placed in a hot air oven at 180◦C for 12 h. The obtained brown solution was washed with Dichloromethane (DCM) to remove the unreacted organic moieties. Then the aqueous layer containing water soluble carbon dots was separated from the organic layer containing the DCM with unreacted organic moieties, using a separating funnel. The obtained yellowish-brown solution was filtered using 0.2 µm filter membrane and stored in an inert container.

Initially, 15 mM stock solution of Quercetin in ethanol was prepared. 20 µL of this solution and 1 µL of aqueous carbon dot solution were added to the cuvette containing 3 ml of deionized water. Alongside, 15 mM Al (NO3)3·9H2O stock solution was prepared independently using deionized water and different concentrations of this solution (5–550 µM) was added to the cuvette containing the above mentioned mixture of quercetin and carbon dots. In each case, fluorescence spectrum was recorded at excitation wavelength 330 nm using excitation and emission slit widths as 5 nm and 5 nm respectively at room temperature.

The surface morphology of carbon dots derived from Citrus hystrix peels was analyzed using HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a & S.2, the prepared CDs possess quasi spherical shape, which is amorphous in nature, thus lacking the lattice fringes. It is found that the formed carbon dots are randomly distributed with its size ranging from 2.5 to 4 nm. The ununiform size distribution of CDs results in excitation dependent emission property.

The FTIR spectroscopy was utilized to infer the functional groups present in the prepared carbon dots. The peak at 3608 cm−1 refers to the O–H bond stretching which justifies the presence of hydroxyl functional groups in the surface of the prepared CDs. The peaks at 2985 cm−1 and 2352 cm−1 are attributed to the C–H bond stretching vibrations and alkyne or isocyanate stretching vibrations respectively [21,22]. A peak at 1535 cm−1 corresponds to the C = C stretching vibrations contributed by the sp2 hybridized carbons of the inner graphitic core of the prepared CDs. This might have resulted from the alkene groups of the essential oils like limonene, sabinene and β- pinene present in the Citrus hystrix peels. On the other hand, the peak at 1685 cm−1 represents the C = O bond stretching in the CDs surface arising from the citronellal which is the major composition of peels.

The elemental composition of the synthesized CDs was assessed using XPS spectroscopy results. The survey scan spectrum in Fig. 1 d illustrates that the CDs were mainly composed of carbon (C1s) and oxygen (O1s) without any trace of impurities. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum in Fig. 1e clearly demonstrates the presence of three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.7 eV which are attributed to C = C, C-O and C = O respectively. The deconvoluted O1s spectrum in Fig. 1f shows two distinctive peaks at 530.8 eV and 532.3 eV which represents C-OH and C = O on the surface of CDs respectively. The results obtained from XPS and FTIR collaboratively confirms that the surface of CDs is comprised of carboxyl functional groups with the graphitic sp2 carbons in the core [23].

The UV– Visible spectrum of the synthesized CDs was recorded using a spectrophotometer and the result is shown in Fig. 2 a. It can be noticed that a broad absorption band, rather than a sharp absorption peak, has appeared which could be attributed to the relatively broad size distribution of CDs [24].The absorption band at 280 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition in the C = C groups of the aromatic sp2 domain of carbon dots. Thus, the blue emission carbon dots with an emission peak at 420 nm have been obtained. The fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared carbon dots was measured as 22 % with reference to aqueous solution of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (λex = 330 nm) using equation (1)
(1)
ϕ =
ϕ R
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
[
η
2
η
R
2
]
Where
ϕ R
is the quantum yield of the reference (quinine sulphate), I is the integrated emission intensity of CD and IR is the integrated emission intensity of reference, A is the optical density and η is the refractive index of the solvent used [25].

As carbon dots are well known for their excitation dependent emission behavior, with a variation in excitation wavelength they tend to possess emission bands at different wavelengths. The emission behavior of synthesized CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 280-370 nm were examined. It was clearly shown in Fig. 2b that the emission bands were slightly shifted to the longer wavelength with a decrease in emission peak intensity. This phenomenon might be ascribed to the factors like varying size of the prepared CDs, random distribution and different organic functional groups present in the CDs [26]. Thus, a strong emission peak with maximum peak intensity was observed at 420 nm when excited at Ex-330 nm.

The emission of carbon dots in the Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) at different pH values ranging from 4 to 9 was examined. It was inferred that the emission intensity of carbon dots increased with the increase in pH from 4 to 7, saturated at pH −7 and decreased with further increase in pH from 8 to 9 (Fig. 2c). The increase in emission intensity at lower pH is due to the protonation of the carboxyl functional groups on the surface of the CDs. Whereas the decrease in emission intensity of carbon dots at higher pH (8 and 9) could be attributed to the increased electronegativity of the surface carboxyl moieties which is due to deprotonation of the surface functional groups [27,28].

Quercetin (QT), the most addressed flavonoid possesses three possible chelating sites which are capable of coordinating with Al3+ effectively. The absorption of QT and QT-Al3+ was analyzed using a UV spectrophotometer and the results are shown in Fig. 2d. About 50 µM concentration of quercetin in ethanol was added to the cuvette containing 3 ml of deionized water and the absorption was monitored. It was observed that QT shows two broad absorption bands at 280 and 385 nm contributed by the benzoyl ring and the cinnamoyl ring present in the QT respectively. The band at 385 nm resulted by the n-π* transition of the cinnamoyl ring in QT. An additional UV absorption band around 430 nm was observed with the introduction of Al3+ to QT. This absorption band confirms the formation of QT-Al3+ complex which strongly overlaps with the emission region of the carbon dots and contributes to the efficient quenching of CDs. This phenomenon results in the Inner Filter effect (IFE) between QT-Al3+ and the CDs. Thus, CD-QT combined system enables the construction of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

In order to detect Aluminium ions, 100 µM of QT in ethanol solution and different concentrations of Al3+ (0–300 µM) were added to a cuvette containing 3 ml of deionized water and a series of emission spectra were recorded upon excitation at 330 nm. QT by itself does not possess any strong fluorescence emission, but the addition of Al3+ led to the occurrence of a new emission peak at 485 nm as a result of formation of QT-Al3+ complex. Fig. 3 a indicates that the emission peak intensity at 485 nm increases gradually with the increase in Al3+concentration. QT is known to possess three possible chelating sites such as 3-hydroxychromone, 5-hydroxy chromone and 3′4′-dihydroxyl groups which could readily form a complex with Al3+ when comes in contact with it. The other possible interactions like internal hydrogen bonding or intramolecular proton transfer between the 3-hydroxy group and the nearer carbonyl group of the 3-hydroxychromone or 5-hydroxy chromone (chromone rings) in the excited state have been inhibited by complexing with Al3+ ions. In Fig. 3b, the concentration of Al3+ against F-485 was plotted and it was found to owe a linear response range over 0–300 µM with R2 = 0.97095 and the lowest detection limit was deduced as 3.1 µM. The limit of detection was calculated using the formula 3*σ/m, where σ stands for standard deviation and m is the slope. Though, QT itself is capable to detect Al3+, the lowest detection limit obtained is higher than the permissible limit of Al3+ fixed by WHO. This is due to the weak fluorescence emission of QT. In addition, the single wavelength-based QT sensor is prone to strong interference effect. Thus, to overcome the flaws possessed by QT system, a ratiometric sensor was constructed using CD and QT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric and colorimetric detection of Al3+ using CD-QT system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the ratiometric detection, Al3+ was added to the mixture of 1 µL of CDs and 100 µM QT and the emission spectra were recorded at Ex- 330 nm. The results in Fig. 3c indicate that the blue emission peak at 420 nm contributed by carbon dot quenched dramatically while a new peak at 480 nm arises simultaneously as the result of the formation of QT-Al3+ complex. Subsequently, different concentrations (5–550 µM) of Al3+ solution was added and the resulting variations in the emission peak maximum at 420 nm and 480 nm were recorded. The fluorescence emitted by carbon dots at 420 nm has been systematically quenched upon the addition of quercetin to it and further addition of Al3+ led to a rapid formation of QT-Al3+ complex by inhibiting the intramolecular proton transfer in the excited state of QT. This complex formation led to emergence of the green emission peak at 480 nm which could be evidenced by naked eye through colour change of the solution from colourless to yellow. The gradual change of the colourless solution (CD-QT system) to bright yellow with the increasing Al3+ concentration displayed in (Fig. 3e) enables colorimetric detection of Al3+. This ratiometric detection system was governed by the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) mechanism which was evidenced from the wide overlap of the absorption of QT-Al3+ with the emission region of CD as shown in S.1a. The strong emission of CD and the subsequent Inner filter effect of CD-QT system endowed a better detection of Al3+ than the individual QT which possess very weak fluorescence comparatively. Thus, the CD-QT system paved the way to construct an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of Al3+ over a wide detection range. The emission peak intensity at 480 nm increases with the increase in concentration of Al3+ and a plot (Fig. 3d) drawn between concentration of Al3+and the emission intensities F480/F420 yielded a straight line over the range of 5–550 µM with R2 = 0.99079. The limit of detection of Al3+ was deduced to be 0.695 µM. The gained results using CD-QT system have been compared with the similar works published in the literature that are presented in (Table.1 ). It was inferred that the CD-QT system exhibited a better linear response and enables broader detection range towards the detection of Al3+ when compared to other reported sensors.

Zinc is the most expected interfering metal ion due to presence of QT in the detection system [7,8]. The absorption of QT-Al3+ is much greater than that of QT-Zn2+ due to better complexing of QT with Al3+. Therefore, the overlap of absorption spectrum of QT-Al3+ with emission spectrum of CDs is maximum. This results in increased emission intensity with the introduction of CDs to the QT-Al3+ system when compared to QT-Zn2+, hence leaving interference of Zn2+ negligible as shown in Fig. 4 b. Thus, comparatively CD with QT system shows better selectivity towards Al3+ avoiding the interfering influence of Zn2+ rather than QT alone in the sensing system.

Subsequently, 50 µM of nitrates of each Fe2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, and Cu2+ salts in deionized water solutions were added to the CD-QT system, and the emission spectra was examined upon excitation at 330 nm. From Fig. 4c, it was noted that only Al3+ forms complex effectively with QT which is evident through the appearance of a new peak at 480 nm, whereas other metal ions are not capable to effectively coordinate with QT to form a complex. Thus, in the presence of other metal ions, quenching of CD emission by the QT only occurred at 420 nm. Therefore, it did not show any interfering effect in the emission region (480 nm) of QT-Al3+ complex. In agreement with the results, only when Al3+ was added to the system, the colourless solution turned green whereas, the addition of other metal ions to the CD-QT system did not show any colour change of solution. These results clearly indicate the superiority of the synthesized CD-QT system towards selective detection of Al3+ by ignoring most of the potential interfering ions including Zinc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of sensor in real sample analysis
Tap water analysis by standard addition method
Al3+ residuals in wheat grains from aluminium phosphide (fumigant)
ce:section.ce:para: Extensive usage of aluminium phosphide fumigant to preserve wheat on large scale warehouses is expected to leave a considerable amount of Al3+ in wheat products such as wheat fluor and bran. In order to evaluate the potential Al3+ contamination in these products, we have investigated the grains after treating it with the commercial (Celphos) aluminium phosphide powder using the developed CD-QT system. The greyish coloured aluminium phosphide powder (1 mg) was added to 10 g of wheat grains and left undisturbed in a ventilated atmosphere until the phosphine gas completely evolved, and the residual powder turns white. The white leftover powder which constitutes aluminium hydroxide was well blended on the wheat surface when collected after 72 h. The treated wheat grains were washed with a mixture of deionized water and hydrochloric acid and the resultant solution was collected for further investigation. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded for various concentrations of this solution using CD-QT system. The results (Fig. 5b) display the appearance of a new peak at 480 nm and the intensity increased with the successive addition of solution. The concentration of Al3+ from the pesticide was quantified and is showcased in ST.1. This result indicates the application of fabricated sensor for quanlity control of wheat products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Al3+ leached into water during cooking
ce:section.ce:para: In order to quantify Al3+ leaching during cooking and packaging processes using aluminium foil, the real sample was prepared by heating 250 ml of deionized water with crushed pieces of aluminium foil (thickness: 11 µm) in a household pressure cooker (Pigeon Stovekraft Aluminium Pressure Cooker, 3L capacity). Various cooking periods have been chosen by counting the number of whistles released with respect to increasing temperature and pressure. According to the manufacturer, a high pressure of about 2.0 bar will be developed inside the cooker at the boiling water temperature of 120℃. In every experiment, cooker was subjected to heating until it releases a fixed number of whistles, then the cooker was allowed to cool down to room temperature, and the resulting water sample from cooker was collected. The collected specimen was introduced to CD-QT system and the fluorescence emission spectra wererecorded by exciting at Ex-330 nm in order to detect any traces of Al3+ leached into the water. It was inferred that there were no traces of Al3+ leaching when the cooker was heated until it released 2 whistles in about 10 minutes. Then, the water and aluminium foil mixture were further subjected to prolonged heating for about 15 and 20 min i.e. until the release of 4 and 6 whistles respectively. The water samples collected after 15 min was introduced to the CD-QT system and the obtained emission spectra was shown in Fig. 5c. The results clearly revealed that the Al3+ leaches from the foil which was indicated by the emergence of the new peak at 480 nm due to the formation of QT-Al3+ complex. As the concentration of the aluminium increased upon each addition of the more and more collected water sample, the ratiometric peak at 480 nm gradually increased along with the colour change of the cuvette solution to yellow. Similarly, the fluorescence emission spectra of water sample collected after 20 min (Fig. 5d) was studied. The emission intensity soared steeply at 480 nm with each addition of collected water sample into the CD-QT system. This impliess that the leaching of Al3+ increases due to prolonged exposure to heat and pressure. The concentration of Al3+ leached from the foil into water at each addition was quantified and the results are showcased in (Table 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Al3+ leached into water during cooking in acidic condition
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that under acidic conditions, Al3+ leaching into food is high due to easy complex formation of citric acid with Al3+ [29]. In an attempt to detect the amount of Al3+ leaching under acidic conditions, 250 ml of water with crushed aluminium foil is heated in the presence of 1.5 ml lemon juice under varying temperature and pressure by heating the cooker for 15 and 20 min respectively. Fluorescence emissions of the collected water sample were recorded (Fig. 5e & f). It was noted that the presence of citric acid led to a drastic increase in emission intensity of CD at 420 nm due to the formation of aluminium citrate which indicates the excessive leaching of Al3+ in acidic conditions. As a result of the heavy overlap of absorption of CD with that of citric acid, the reabsorption of CD emission by aluminium citrate contributes Inner Filter Effect mechanism. In addition, there was a pronounced shift towards the red wavelength with the increase in the concentration of water sample indicating the increase in polarity of the system due to the introduction of citric acid. The titration data depicted in (Fig. 5e) shows that the aluminium is strongly chelated to citrate when compared with QT, and thus no peak was observed at 480 nm which occurred in the absence of citric acid. Thus, the fabricated sensor can be effectively utilized for monitoring Al3+ leaching into water under various conditions.

A ratiometric fluorescent probe was developed using carbon dots derived from citrus hystrix peels and quercetin. The CD-QT system exhibited green fluorescence emission in the presence of Al3+. With the increasing concentrations of Al3+ to the system, there was a visible colour change of the solution into yellow as a result of the formation of QT-Al3+ complex. This colour change of the solution indicates the applicability of the CD-QT system for qualitative monitoring of Al3+ leaching. This probe works based on the Inner Filter Effect mechanism for detection of Al3+ with a broad detection range from 5 to 550 µM and the lowest detection limit of 0.695 µM. The fabricated sensor engendered good selectivity, sensitivity and better anti-interference property during detection of Al3+, completely forbidding the interference of Zn2+. The constructed fluorescence system was used for real sample analyses such as laboratory tap water, aluminium foil used in food packaging and pesticide. It was also evidenced that there was excessive leaching of aluminium in presence of citric acid. In addition, the application of the sensor for Al3+ detection in wheat has been demonstrated. The fabricated sensor stands as a precise analytical tool for detection of aluminium leaching into food and thus, meets the demand for construction of a robust tool for Al3+ detection.
Archana Pandi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sekar Chinnathambi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: C. Sekar reports financial support was provided by Alagappa University Research Fellowship.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to MHRD-RUSA 2.0, (No. A1/375/Spectro/BEBS/RUSA/19, dated 25.11.2019). The author PA acknowledges the AURF Fellowship/2022, dt. 22.03.2022 for financial assistance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2024.113696.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: fx1Archana Pandi received her B.Sc., degree in Chemistry in 2018 from V.V. Vanniaperumal College, Virudhunagar affiliated to Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, India. She received her M.Sc., in Chemistry in 2020 from Thiagaraja College, affiliated to Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, India. She is a fulltime Ph.D., scholar under the supervision of Dr. C. Sekar at the Department of Bioelectronics and Biosensors, Alagappa University, Karaikudi, India. Her research work interest is focused on the synthesis of biomass derived carbon quantum dots based fluorescent sensors for food, environmental and agricultural applications.

fx2Sekar Chinnathambi received his Ph.D. degree in Material Science from Anna University, Chennai in the year 1997. He served as post-doctoral researcher at NTT Corporation, Japan and IFW Dresden, Germany for about 8 years. He has received Sir C.V Raman research innovative award. Currently he is a Senior Professor and Head of the Department of Bioelectronics and Biosensors at Alagappa University, Karaikudi. His research activities include chemo-biosensors for medical, food and environmental applications, nanostructured metal oxides, biomaterials, carbon nanotubes and low dimensional cuprates.
